resources.string: Pratham-Chikitse
Emergency First Aid Guide
🔊 START AUDIO
🔇 STOP AUDIO
✅ Do\'s
❌ Don\'ts
Nearest Hospitals
Swipe to next step →
Step
of
📞 Call 108 – Ambulance
✅ Works Offline
Select an Emergency
Distance
Phone
Call Now
🐍 Snake Bite
❤️ Heart Attack
🔥 Burn
😮 Choking
🦴 Fracture
🌊 Drowning
⚡ Electric Shock
🧠 Seizure
☠️ Poisoning
👁️ Eye Injury
🌡️ High Fever
💨 Asthma Attack
🦵 Sprain
🩸 Nosebleed
💉 Diabetic Emergency
🤕 Head Injury
🚨 Allergic Reaction
🩹 Severe Bleeding
☀️ Heatstroke
😴 Unconscious Person
Keep the person calm and still. Immobilize the bitten limb.
Remove rings, watches, or tight clothing near the bite.
Do NOT cut the wound or try to suck out the venom.
Position the bitten limb below the level of the heart.
Gently wash the wound with soap and clean water.
Do NOT apply a tourniquet or ice to the bite.
Rush to the nearest hospital for anti-venom treatment immediately.
Take note of the snake\'s appearance to describe to the doctor.
Do NOT give the person alcohol or any medication.
Call emergency services (108) immediately. Every second counts!
Have the person sit or lie down in a comfortable position.
Do NOT leave the person alone at any time.
Loosen any tight clothing around the neck and chest.
If prescribed, help them take their nitroglycerin or aspirin.
Do NOT give food or water to the person.
If the person becomes unconscious, begin CPR if trained to do so.
Stay calm and reassure the person until help arrives.
Do NOT attempt to drive them yourself; wait for the ambulance.
Cool the burn immediately under cool (not cold) running water for 10–20 minutes.
Remove jewelry and clothing near the burn area before swelling starts.
Do NOT use ice, butter, toothpaste, or any home remedy on the burn.
Cover the burn loosely with a sterile non-stick bandage or clean cloth.
Give over-the-counter pain relief like paracetamol if available.
Do NOT break any blisters that form on the burned skin.
Seek medical attention for burns larger than a palm, burns on face/hands/genitals, or deep burns.
Keep the person warm to prevent shock while waiting for help.
Do NOT wrap the burn tightly — keep the covering loose.
Ask "Are you choking?" If they cannot speak/breathe, act immediately.
Lean the person forward and give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades.
Do NOT perform back blows if the person can still cough forcefully.
Give 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich Maneuver): stand behind, make fist above navel, pull sharply inward and upward.
Alternate between 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object is dislodged.
Do NOT perform abdominal thrusts on infants or pregnant women. Use chest thrusts instead.
If the person loses consciousness, lower them carefully to the floor and call 108. Begin CPR if trained.
If object becomes visible in the mouth, carefully remove it with a finger sweep.
Do NOT perform blind finger sweeps — only remove object if clearly visible.
Keep the injured area still. Do not try to straighten the bone.
Immobilize the fracture using a splint (board, rolled newspaper) padded with cloth.
Do NOT move the person if you suspect a spine or neck fracture.
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling and pain.
Elevate the injured limb above heart level if possible to reduce swelling.
Do NOT try to push a protruding bone back in.
Seek immediate medical help. For open fractures (bone visible), cover with clean cloth and call 108.
Keep the patient calm and still. Check for pulse and sensation below the injury.
Do NOT give the person food or water in case surgery is needed.
Ensure YOUR safety first. Do not jump in water unless trained — throw a rope, branch, or float object.
Call 108 immediately. Get the person out of water as safely and quickly as possible.
Do NOT enter deep water without training — you risk becoming a second victim.
Once out, lay the person on their back. Check for breathing. If not breathing, begin rescue breaths.
Give 2 rescue breaths: tilt head back, lift chin, cover mouth, and blow for 1 second each.
Do NOT waste time trying to drain water from lungs — focus on breathing and CPR.
Begin CPR: 30 chest compressions (push hard and fast on center of chest) followed by 2 rescue breaths.
Continue CPR until help arrives or the person starts breathing normally.
Do NOT leave the person alone once rescued — keep monitoring closely.
DO NOT touch the person if they are still in contact with electricity. Switch off the power source first.
Use a non-conducting object (dry wood, rubber) to move the power source away if the switch cannot be reached.
Do NOT touch the person with bare hands while they are in contact with electricity.
Once safe, call 108. Check if the person is breathing and conscious.
If not breathing, begin CPR immediately. Electric shock can stop the heart.
Do NOT apply water to an electrical burn.
Treat burns from the shock: cool with running water and cover loosely.
Keep the person lying flat and warm. Watch for delayed shock symptoms.
Do NOT leave the person unattended — internal injuries may not be immediately visible.
Stay calm. Time the seizure. Clear the area of any hard or sharp objects.
Gently place something soft under the person\'s head. Turn them on their side if possible.
Do NOT restrain the person or hold them down during the seizure.
Stay with the person throughout the seizure. Keep bystanders away.
After the seizure, place the person in the recovery position (on their side) to prevent choking.
Do NOT put anything in the person\'s mouth — they cannot swallow their tongue.
Call 108 if: seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, person doesn\'t wake up, or it\'s a first-ever seizure.
Speak calmly to the person as they recover. Confusion after a seizure is normal.
Do NOT give food or water until the person is fully conscious and aware.
Call 108 or the poison control center immediately. Do NOT wait for symptoms to appear.
Try to identify the poison: chemical name, quantity, and time of exposure.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by medical professionals.
If poison is on skin or eyes, rinse with large amounts of cool water for 15–20 minutes.
If the person is unconscious but breathing, place them in the recovery position.
Do NOT give milk, water, or any other substance to drink unless instructed.
Bring the poison container or substance to the hospital for identification by doctors.
Monitor breathing and consciousness. Begin CPR if breathing stops.
Do NOT leave the person alone. Keep them calm and still.
Do NOT rub the eye. Keep the person still and calm.
For chemical splash: rinse the eye immediately with clean water for 15–20 minutes.
Do NOT try to remove an object embedded in the eye.
For a foreign particle: blink rapidly or rinse with clean water. Pull the upper eyelid over the lower one.
Cover the eye loosely with a clean cloth or eye patch to prevent further injury.
Do NOT apply pressure to an injured eye or put any drops without medical guidance.
Seek medical attention immediately for any chemical burn, penetrating injury, or sudden vision change.
Keep both eyes covered (covering one reduces movement of the injured eye).
Do NOT remove contact lenses if the eye is chemically injured — flush with contacts in place first.
Measure temperature. A fever above 103°F (39.4°C) requires immediate attention.
Apply a cool, damp cloth to the forehead, armpits, and groin to bring down temperature.
Do NOT use an ice bath or very cold water — this can cause shivering and raise temperature.
Keep the person hydrated. Encourage frequent sips of water, ORS, or coconut water.
Give appropriate fever-reducing medicine like Paracetamol as per age and dosage instructions.
Do NOT give Aspirin to children — it can cause a dangerous condition called Reye\'s syndrome.
Seek immediate medical help for fever with stiff neck, rash, difficulty breathing, or seizures.
Keep the room well-ventilated. Light, breathable clothing is preferred.
Do NOT wrap the person in heavy blankets — this traps heat and worsens fever.
Help the person sit upright and lean slightly forward. This position opens the airways.
Help them use their reliever inhaler (usually blue). Give one puff every 30–60 seconds up to 10 puffs.
Do NOT lay the person flat — this makes breathing harder.
Help them breathe slowly and steadily. Encourage pursed-lip breathing.
Loosen any tight clothing around their neck and chest.
Do NOT leave the person alone during an asthma attack.
Call 108 immediately if: the inhaler has no effect after 10 puffs, lips turn blue, or they can\'t speak.
Keep calm and reassure them — anxiety makes asthma attacks worse.
Do NOT allow the person to exercise or exert themselves during or just after an attack.
Rest the injured area immediately. Stop any activity causing pain.
Apply Ice wrapped in cloth for 20 minutes every 2 hours for the first 48 hours.
Do NOT apply heat or massage in the first 48 hours — this increases swelling.
Compress the area with a bandage to reduce swelling. Not too tight!
Elevate the injured limb above heart level to reduce swelling.
Do NOT bear weight on a severe sprain until cleared by a doctor.
Seek medical attention if: you cannot bear weight, extreme swelling, numbness, or the bone looks deformed.
Take over-the-counter pain relief like ibuprofen or paracetamol if available.
Do NOT try to \"walk off\" a severe sprain — you may be dealing with a fracture.
Have the person sit upright and lean slightly FORWARD (not backward).
Pinch the soft part of the nose firmly and breathe through the mouth.
Do NOT tilt the head backward — blood can go into the throat and cause choking.
Maintain pressure for 10–15 minutes without releasing. Apply a cold compress to the bridge of the nose.
After bleeding stops, avoid blowing nose for several hours.
Do NOT pack the nose tightly with cotton — this can damage tissue.
Seek medical help if: bleeding doesn\'t stop after 30 minutes, caused by head injury, or person is on blood thinners.
Keep the person calm and still — anxiety raises blood pressure and worsens bleeding.
Do NOT blow nose immediately after bleeding stops — this can restart the bleed.
Identify if the person has high or low blood sugar. Low sugar (Hypoglycemia) is more immediately dangerous.
For LOW blood sugar: give 15g of fast-acting sugar — 3–4 glucose tablets, or 150ml fruit juice, or sugar dissolved in water.
Do NOT give food or drink to an unconscious person — they can choke.
Wait 15 minutes and check symptoms. If no improvement, give another dose of sugar and call 108.
If the person is unconscious, place them in the recovery position and call 108 immediately.
Do NOT give insulin for low blood sugar — this will make it worse.
For HIGH blood sugar (Hyperglycemia): encourage drinking water and seek medical help promptly.
Stay with the person and monitor their level of consciousness until help arrives.
Do NOT assume it is always low sugar — only give sugar if you are certain it is hypoglycemia.
Keep the person still and calm. Do NOT move them unless they are in danger.
Stop any bleeding by applying gentle pressure with a clean cloth. Do NOT press on the skull if you suspect a fracture.
Do NOT remove a helmet or head protection if a fracture is suspected.
Watch for warning signs: loss of consciousness, confusion, vomiting, seizures, unequal pupils, or slurred speech.
If unconscious but breathing, gently place in the recovery position. Stabilize the head and neck.
Do NOT give aspirin or ibuprofen — they increase bleeding. Paracetamol is safer.
Call 108 immediately for any serious head injury. Even mild concussions require medical evaluation.
Keep the person awake and talking. Monitor every 30 minutes for next 24 hours.
Do NOT leave the person alone or allow them to sleep immediately after a head injury.
Identify and remove the allergen source if possible (food, insect, medication).
If the person has an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen), use it immediately in the outer thigh.
Do NOT give antihistamines as primary treatment for a severe reaction (anaphylaxis).
Call 108 immediately for severe reactions: difficulty breathing, throat swelling, drop in blood pressure, loss of consciousness.
Have the person lie flat with legs elevated (unless breathing is difficult — then sit them up).
Do NOT leave the person alone after using the EpiPen — effects are temporary and reaction can return.
Perform CPR if the person stops breathing while waiting for ambulance.
Note the allergen and time for the medical team. A second EpiPen dose may be needed after 5–15 minutes.
Do NOT assume they are recovering if symptoms temporarily improve — they need hospital care.
Apply firm, direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth, bandage, or clothing.
Press firmly and continuously without releasing for at least 10–15 minutes.
Do NOT remove the cloth if it soaks through — add more layers on top and keep pressing.
If an arm or leg is bleeding severely and pressure does not stop it, apply a tourniquet above the wound.
Tourniquet: tie tight cloth 5–8cm above wound, twist until bleeding stops, note the time applied.
Do NOT use a tourniquet for neck, chest, or abdominal wounds. Apply pressure only.
Keep the person lying down. Elevate the injured part above heart level. Call 108 immediately.
Keep the person warm and calm. Monitor for signs of shock: pale skin, rapid breathing, confusion.
Do NOT give the person food or water — they may need surgery.
Move the person to a cool, shaded, or air-conditioned area immediately.
Remove excess clothing. Cool the person rapidly: spray with cool water and fan them.
Do NOT give anything to drink if the person is confused, unconscious, or vomiting.
Apply ice packs or cool wet cloths to neck, armpits, and groin — areas with major blood vessels.
If conscious and coherent, give cool water or a sports drink to sip slowly.
Do NOT give aspirin or paracetamol for heatstroke — they are not effective and may cause harm.
Call 108. Heatstroke is life-threatening. Keep cooling the person until help arrives.
If the person becomes unconscious, place in recovery position and monitor breathing.
Do NOT leave the person in a hot environment for even a moment.
Check for response: tap shoulders, shout "Are you okay?" If no response, call 108 immediately.
Check for breathing: look for chest rise, listen, and feel for breath for 10 seconds.
Do NOT move the person if a spinal injury is suspected (fall from height, vehicle accident).
If breathing: Place in the Recovery Position — on their side, head tilted back, top knee bent forward for stability.
Keep the airway open. Monitor breathing every minute until help arrives.
Do NOT give anything by mouth to an unconscious person.
If NOT breathing: Begin CPR immediately. 30 chest compressions, then 2 rescue breaths. Repeat until help arrives.
If an AED (Defibrillator) is available, use it as soon as possible — follow the voice instructions.
Do NOT leave the person alone at any time until trained help takes over.
